1: massa devar YHWH be’eretz Chadrach veDamaseq menuchato: ‘An oracle — the word of the LORD against the land of Hadrach; Damascus is where it comes to rest.’ Massa (oracle/burden) is the technical term for a prophetic oracle, often against foreign nations (used in Isa 13-23; Nah 1:1). The specific geography — Hadrach (Hatarikka in Assyrian records, north of Damascus), Damascus, Hamath, Tyre, Sidon — traces the Syro-Phoenician coastal region from north to south, then turns to Philistia in vv.5-7. This geographical progression from north to south mirrors the approach of Alexander the Great’s campaign (332 BCE), leading many scholars to see it as a prophecy of Alexander’s conquests — a reading that would place the oracle’s “fulfillment” in 332 BCE before the ultimate fulfillment in Christ.
2: vegam Chamath tigbal bah Tzor veTzidon ki chakemah me’od: ‘And also Hamath, which borders Damascus, Tyre and Sidon, though they are very clever.’ The clause ki chakemah me’od (though they are very clever/wise) is ironic: the legendary wisdom and commercial acumen of Tyre and Sidon (Ezek 28:3-5 develops this at length) cannot prevent YHWH’s judgment. The same ironic treatment of “wisdom” (human cleverness that cannot outmaneuver divine judgment) appears in 1 Corinthians 1:20-25: “Where is the one who is wise?...God has made foolish the wisdom of the world.”
3: vativen Tzor matsor lah vatatzbar kesef ke’afar vecharutz kimid ulechot: ‘Tyre has built herself a fortress and amassed silver like dust and gold like the mud of the streets.’ The accumulation language (vatatzbar, she has heaped up) and the extreme comparisons (silver like dust, gold like mud) describe the hyperbolic wealth of Tyre, the Mediterranean’s greatest trading port. The contrast between the extraordinary wealth of accumulated trade (kesef ke’afar, silver like dust) and the dismissive terms in which it is compared (dust, mud of streets) undercuts the apparent permanence of commercial wealth. Ezekiel 28 develops the Tyre oracle into the most extensive biblical meditation on pride born of commercial success.
4: hineh Adonay yoreshennah vehikah bayam cheilah vehi ba’esh te’akel: ‘But look — the Lord will dispossess her and hurl her wealth into the sea, and she will be consumed by fire.’ The sudden divine reversal — all that accumulated wealth hurled into the sea from which it came — is YHWH’s judgment turning Tyre’s commercial identity against her. The sea that made Tyre wealthy becomes the receptacle of her destruction. This pattern (a power’s defining feature becoming the instrument of its judgment) is consistent throughout the Prophets: the sea-people destroyed by the sea; the shepherd-king struck; the temple destroyed.
5: tir’eh Ashqelon vetira Gaza tigel me’od ve’Eqron ki hovish miqvavetah: ‘Ashkelon will see it and be terrified; Gaza too, writhing in anguish; and Ekron, for its hope has withered.’ The three Philistine cities’ reaction to Tyre’s fall — fear, anguish, withered hope — is the chain reaction of psychological collapse when a great regional power falls: those who depended on its trade networks and military protection suddenly find themselves exposed. Miqveh (hope, literally that which is expected/awaited) withering for Ekron means its anticipated future of prosperity evaporates when the patron city falls.
6: veyashav mamzer be’Ashdod vehichrati ge’on Pelishtim: ‘A mixed people will settle in Ashdod, and I will cut off Philistia’s pride.’ Mamzer (literally “illegitimate/mixed” — a person of uncertain or mixed parentage) settling in Ashdod signifies the disruption of Philistia’s ethnic and cultural cohesion — the city will be populated by displaced, mixed populations rather than the proud Philistine aristocracy. Ge’on (pride, majesty, arrogance) is cut off — the same term used of Israel’s pride (Amos 6:8; Hos 5:5) and of the pride that YHWH destroys throughout the Prophets. Pride is consistently the divine target in judgments on both nations and individuals.
7: vehesiroti demav mipiv veshuqtzav mibeyn shenav venish’ar gam hu leEloheinu: ‘I will strip the blood from its mouth and the forbidden offerings from between its teeth. Even they will belong to our God.’ The remarkable inclusion at the end of the verse — the formerly pagan Philistines belonging to YHWH — is the eschatological surprise of the oracle. Blood in the mouth and forbidden food between the teeth represent Philistia’s cultic impurity; their removal is the purification that enables inclusion. The comparison to a “clan leader in Judah” and Ekron becoming “like the Jebusites” (who were absorbed into Israel after David captured Jerusalem) shows that Gentile incorporation into the covenant community is the oracle’s endpoint.
8: vechaniti leveti mitzava me’over ume’shav velo ya’avor aleihem od nages: ‘I will take up position around my house as a garrison, so that no army marches through it again. No oppressor will march over them, for now my eyes watch over them.’ YHWH as garrison around his house — the divine protection of the restored community through YHWH himself acting as military guard. The promise ki atta ra’iti be’einay (for now I watch with my eyes) is the assurance of divine surveillance and care replacing the vulnerability that made conquest possible. This verse is structurally the pivot between the judgment oracles against the nations (vv.1-7) and the messianic announcement (vv.9-10) — YHWH secures his house so the king can come.
9: gili me’od bat Tzion hari’i bat Yerushalayim hineh malkeich yavo lach tzaddiq venoshia hu ani verocheiv al chamor ve’al ‘ayir ben atenot: ‘Rejoice greatly, Daughter Zion! Shout aloud, Daughter Jerusalem! See — your king is coming to you, righteous and having-salvation, humble and riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a she-donkey.’ The two Hebrew terms require careful analysis: (1) tzaddiq (righteous): the king is intrinsically righteous — this is a character attribute, not merely a status. (2) venoshia: the Niphal form of yasha (to save), meaning “being-saved” or “having-salvation” — this is the rarer reading. The king does not save himself; he is saved by YHWH, or he is one who has/carries salvation from YHWH. The contrast between the war-horse (sus) and the donkey (chamor) + colt (‘ayir + ben atenot) signals the nature of his kingdom: a king on a war-horse signals conquest; a king on a donkey signals peace and accessibility. Ani (humble/poor/afflicted) is the same term used in Psalm 22:24 of the one whose cry YHWH heard — the paradigmatic sufferer-and-redeemed.
10: vehikrati rechev me’Efraim vesus miYerushalayim venikretah qeshet milchamah vedibbar shalom lagoyim umoshelo miyam ad yam uminarahot ad afsei aretz: ‘He will cut off the chariot from Ephraim and the war horse from Jerusalem; the battle bow will be cut off. He will proclaim peace to the nations. His dominion will be from sea to sea and from the River to the ends of the earth.’ The disarmament by the messianic king — he removes the chariot, horse, and bow from the covenant community — signals the arrival of the eschatological peace age. Vediber shalom lagoyim (he will speak/proclaim peace to the nations): the king’s speech itself establishes peace, not merely announces it. The universal dominion formula — miyam ad yam (from sea to sea) + minarahot ad afsei aretz (from the River to the ends of the earth) — echoes Psalm 72:8, the royal psalm of ideal Davidic reign now applied to the messianic king whose kingdom encompasses all.
11: gam at bedam beritayich shilachti asirayich mibbor ein maim bo: ‘As for you — because of the blood of my covenant with you — I will free your prisoners from the waterless pit.’ Dam beritayich (blood of my covenant with you) — the blood of the Sinai covenant (Exod 24:8: “this is the blood of the covenant that YHWH has made with you”) is the basis for the liberation of prisoners. The bor ein maim (waterless pit) echoes Joseph’s pit (Gen 37:24: the pit had no water in it) and Jeremiah’s cistern (Jer 38:6). The contrast between “waterless pit” and “fortress of hope” (v.12) is the liberation movement from imprisonment to security. Jesus quotes this verse’s liberation theme (Luke 4:18: “to proclaim liberty to the captives”) and the “blood of the covenant” becomes the central phrase of the Last Supper (Matt 26:28: “this is my blood of the covenant”).
12: shuvah levitzaron asirei hatiqvah gam hayom maggid mishneh asiv lach: ‘Return to the stronghold, you prisoners who still have hope; today I declare that I will restore double to you.’ Bitzaron (stronghold/fortress) — the secure place of refuge that YHWH promises to the hopeful prisoners. Asirei hatiqvah (prisoners of hope) is a beautiful paradox: those who are imprisoned yet have hope (in YHWH’s promise). The double restoration (mishneh) echoes the Job pattern (Job 42:10: YHWH restored Job’s fortunes double) and the Isaiah promise (Isa 40:2: Jerusalem has received double for all her sins; 61:7: you will receive a double portion instead of shame). The return to the stronghold — from waterless pit to fortress — is the rescue movement accomplished by the blood-of-covenant liberation.
13: ki darakhti li Yehudah qeshet milleti Efraim ve’orerti vanayich Tziyon al vanayich Yavan: ‘For I have bent Judah like a bow; I have made Ephraim its arrow. I will rouse your sons, Zion, against the sons of Greece.’ YHWH himself as the one who bends the bow and nocks the arrow — using Judah and Ephraim as weapons — is the divine-warrior imagery at its most concrete. The specific mention of Yavan (Greece/Javan) is one of the only explicit references to the Greek world in the Hebrew prophets, and may point to the Maccabean period or be deliberately eschatological. The combat between the sons of Zion and the sons of Greece is a historical specificity that becomes the broader conflict between the covenant community and the Hellenizing pressures that climaxed in Antiochus Epiphanes’ program.
14: va’Adonay aleyhem yera’eh veyatza chakatzetz baraqo uAdonay Elohim bashofer yitqa vealach bese’arot teman: ‘The LORD will appear over them; his arrow will flash like lightning. The Lord GOD will sound the trumpet and march in the storms of the south.’ The theophanic imagery of divine appearance — lightning-arrow, trumpet-blast, storm-march — is drawn from the Sinai traditions (Exod 19:16-19: thunder, lightning, thick cloud, loud trumpet) and the divine-warrior psalms (Ps 18:14: he sent out his arrows and scattered them; he flashed forth lightnings and routed them). The combination of visual (lightning), auditory (trumpet), and physical (storm from the south) elements creates a comprehensive theophanic announcement of divine intervention on behalf of his people.
15: YHWH tzvaot yagen aleihem ve’achelu vekaveshu even qela’ veshatu hamah kimayim mizraq: ‘The LORD of Hosts will shield them. They will devour and trample the sling stones; they will drink and shout as with wine.’ The vivid battle imagery — devouring sling stones, drinking and shouting — is the OT’s hyperbolic celebration of victorious warriors who are unstoppable because YHWH shields them. The verb yagen (shield, protect, cover) is the word for YHWH as shield throughout the psalms (Ps 18:30: YHWH is a shield to those who take refuge in him). The battle hymn quality of this verse points forward to the eschatological warfare motifs of Revelation 19.
16: vehoshi’am YHWH Eloheihem bayom hahu ketzon ammo ki avnei nezer mitnoseshot al admatho: ‘On that day the LORD their God will save them as his people’s flock. They will gleam like gems in a crown over his land.’ The pastoral/royal double image — saved as a flock (tzon ammo, sheep of his people) but gleaming like gems in a crown (avnei nezer) — combines the shepherd metaphor with the coronation image. The people of YHWH are both sheep (needing a shepherd) and jewels (precious to the king). The word nezer (crown/diadem) is used for the high priest’s golden crown (Exod 29:6) and the royal diadem: the restored people are simultaneously priestly and royal — the description of the eschatological community as “a kingdom of priests” (Exod 19:6; 1 Pet 2:9) applied here.
17: ki mah tuvah umah yofyah dagan bachurim vethirosh yenovev betulot: ‘How great is his goodness, how great is his beauty! Grain will strengthen the young men, and new wine will cheer the young women.’ The exclamation mah tuvah umah yofyah (how great is his goodness, how great is his beauty!) is YHWH’s goodness and beauty celebrated — tuv (goodness, bounty) and yofi (beauty, splendor). The agricultural prosperity that follows — grain for young men, new wine for young women — is the material embodiment of divine goodness. The pairing of dagan (grain) and tirosh (new wine) is the standard covenantal blessing pair (Deut 7:13; 11:14; Hos 2:8), here representing the full restoration of covenant prosperity.
